definition: The shift in stock-bond correlation from negative to positive (+0.15 to +0.35) alters FX hedging calculus for global portfolios. Foreign investors face a triple hit (equity losses + bond losses + potential dollar depreciation), shifting equilibrium hedge ratio from ~50% to ~80-90%. Near-term this supports the dollar through increased hedging demand; medium-term it creates larger forced selling in portfolio reallocation.
evidence: Correlation shift from negative to +0.15 to +0.35
Equilibrium hedge ratio shift from ~50% to ~80-90%
Maturity-dependent bifurcation (2Y-SPX negative vs. 30Y-SPX positive) as transition diagnostic
open_questions: Is the correlation regime shift permanent or cyclical?
At what portfolio allocation threshold does forced selling overwhelm hedging demand?
How does the maturity-dependent bifurcation evolve through the rate cycle?
